raw_text.response:
Yes.  
If an entire function  

\[
f(z)=u(z)+iv(z),\qquad z\in\mathbb C,
\]

satisfies  

\[
|f(z)|\le M\qquad\text{for all }z\in\mathbb C,
\]

then \(f\) must be constant.  
This is the classical **Liouville’s theorem**.  
Below is a short proof that uses only the Cauchy integral formula (the same argument that appears in the documents you retrieved).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Proof

1. **Bound the derivative.**  
   For any \(R>0\) define  

   \[
   f_R(z)=f(Rz),\qquad z\in\mathbb C .
   \]

   Since \(f\) is entire, so is \(f_R\).  
   Apply the Cauchy integral formula for the derivative at the point \(z=0\) on the unit circle \(|w|=1\):

   \[
   f_R'(0)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{|w|=1}\frac{f_R(w)}{w^{2}}\,dw .
   \]

   Taking absolute values and using \(|f_R(w)|=|f(Rw)|\le M\),

   \[
   |f_R'(0)|
   \le\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{|w|=1}\frac{M}{|w|^{2}}\,|dw|
   =M .
   \]

   Because \(f_R'(0)=R\,f'(0)\) (differentiate \(f_R(z)=f(Rz)\) and set \(z=0\)), we obtain  

   \[
   |f'(0)|\le\frac{M}{R}.
   \]

   The same estimate holds at any point \(z_0\) after translating the centre of the circle, so for every \(z\in\mathbb C\),

   \[
   |f'(z)|\le\frac{M}{R}\qquad\text{for every }R>0 .
   \]

2. **Let \(R\to\infty\).**  
   Since the right–hand side can be made arbitrarily small, we must have  

   \[
   f'(z)=0\qquad\text{for all }z\in\mathbb C .
   \]

3. **Conclude constancy.**  
   A holomorphic function whose derivative is identically zero is constant. Hence \(f\) is constant on \(\mathbb C\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
### Remarks

* The same conclusion can be reached by observing that a bounded entire function has a removable singularity at the point \(\infty\); after adding the point \(\infty\) to the complex plane (the Riemann sphere) the function becomes holomorphic on a compact set, and therefore must be constant (see Document 2).

* Even a weaker hypothesis—*only the real part of \(f\) is bounded*—already forces \(f\) to be constant (Document 4). The boundedness of the whole modulus is, of course, stronger.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
**Therefore, the “spell” that works everywhere and never exceeds a fixed temperature (i.e., a bounded entire function) can only produce one fixed temperature change: the function must be constant.**
